note_data.title: Chemical bonding
keyPoints: **Ionic bonding** occurs between metals and non-metals through complete transfer of electrons
**Covalent bonding** occurs between non-metals through sharing of electrons
**Metallic bonding** occurs between metal atoms through a sea of delocalised electrons
Ionic compounds form **giant ionic structures** with high melting and boiling points
Covalent compounds can form **simple molecular structures** or **giant covalent structures**
Metallic compounds form **giant metallic structures** with delocalised electrons
Ionic compounds conduct electricity when **molten or dissolved** but not when solid
Metals conduct electricity due to **mobile delocalised electrons**
Simple molecular covalent compounds have **weak intermolecular forces** between molecules
Giant covalent structures like diamond and graphite have **very high melting points**

definitions.definition: The electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by the complete transfer of electrons from metal atoms to non-metal atoms
The sharing of pairs of electrons between atoms
The electrostatic attraction between metal cations and a sea of delocalised electrons
A three-dimensional arrangement of atoms or ions extending throughout the crystal
Electrons that are not associated with a particular atom but are free to move throughout the structure

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give details about the structure or bonding without explanation

modelAnswer.question: Explain why sodium chloride has a high melting point but does not conduct electricity when solid. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Sodium chloride has a giant ionic structure with strong electrostatic forces between oppositely charged ions, requiring a lot of energy to break. When solid, the ions are in fixed positions and cannot move, so it cannot conduct electricity.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Giant ionic structure/strong electrostatic forces
Mark 2: A lot of energy needed to break forces
Mark 3: Ions in fixed positions/cannot move when solid

commonMistakes: Never say 'atoms' in ionic compounds — always say 'ions'
Students often confuse intermolecular forces (between molecules) with intramolecular forces (within molecules)
Never say metals 'share' electrons — they have delocalised electrons
Don't confuse simple molecular structures with giant covalent structures
Students forget that graphite conducts electricity due to delocalised electrons between layers
examinerTips: This topic appears in almost every Paper 2 exam
Always relate properties to structure and bonding for full marks
Use dot-and-cross diagrams to show electron transfer or sharing
Remember that conductivity questions often ask about molten vs solid states
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Dot-and-cross diagrams showing electron arrangements in ionic compounds (e.g., NaCl, MgO) and covalent molecules (e.g., H2O, CH4). Also include simple diagrams of giant ionic, metallic, and covalent structures.
